textPlain: Summarizes paper "Targeting iron-associated protein Ftl1 in the brain of old mice improves age-related cognitive impairment" [1]Could lowering FTL1 restore synaptic connectivity and memory in old mice represent a master switch in brain aging, or just one of many parallel mechanisms?  If FTL1 is sufficient to induce both structural and functional brain aging in mice, what does that imply about the hierarchy of molecular drivers in neurodegeneration[1] https://www.nature.com/articles/s43587-025-00940-z
This feels like a headline that arrives in my parents' spam folder every two weeks or so for the past two decades.
quick reading on that protein suggests to me, a layman, that more aerobic exercise and less red meat should be helping here against such brain aging. Sounds so familiar.
